channel.title: Feedly Digest
channel.description: Current article list from the Feedly digest on maraz.sk.

item.title: [Informatics] AI agenti: bezpečnostní průser, na který nemá ani Anthropic odpověď
item.description:
The article walks through prompt injection as a structural security problem for agents connected to email, files, calendars, and payment-capable tools. It is useful because it separates statistical model defenses from deterministic system boundaries and treats connectors, memory, and tool access as the real risk multipliers.

  • Agent risk changes when untrusted text can influence tools with real authority.
  • Prompt-injection filtering is probabilistic; the system design still needs least privilege, explicit trust boundaries, and confirmation for dangerous actions.
  • Persistent memory and connected inboxes create attack paths that can survive beyond one chat session.

item.title: [Informatics] Fixed-Height Cards: More Fragile Than They Look
item.description:
The piece shows how fixed card heights, line clamps, absolute-positioned actions, and guessed padding create brittle layouts that fail under translation, missing images, larger fonts, and narrow viewports. The better pattern is intrinsic sizing, grid alignment, flex column flow, and explicit stress tests for real content.

  • A card layout that works only because overflow is hidden is already under strain.
  • Let grid and flexbox handle alignment instead of hard-coding pixel ceilings inside components.
  • Stress-test components with long words, larger text, wrapped controls, missing media, and narrow widths before treating them as stable.

item.title: [Programming] High Performance Git
item.description:
This free book treats Git as a content-addressed database, filesystem cache, graph walker, and transfer protocol, then works through the performance costs of objects, refs, indexes, packfiles, commit graphs, sparse checkouts, partial clones, protocol v2, and repository recovery.

  • Git performance problems usually come from storage shape, history traversal, ref scale, transfer policy, or index cost rather than one slow command.
  • Large repos need commit-graphs, Bloom filters, multi-pack indexes, bitmaps, sparse index, partial clone, and scheduled maintenance as design tools.
  • The material is directly relevant for monorepos, CI, developer-experience work, and debugging slow local Git operations.

item.title: [Programming] Why you can't allocate a human-readable ID before the record exists
item.description:
The article uses a task-key system to show why human-readable identifiers must be assigned atomically with record creation, not reserved before the durable object exists. It turns a small product feature into a concrete lesson about idempotency keys, retries, reapers, and partial failure.

  • The idempotency key should gate the reservation, while the public number should be assigned only when the real object commits.
  • Pending allocation rows need a reconciliation job because clients cannot be trusted to retry forever.
  • A clean human-facing sequence such as SYN-42 still needs failure-mode design behind it.

item.title: [Philosophy] Pluralistic: The three armies fighting for the post-American world
item.description:
Doctorow frames a post-American internet as a coalition between digital-rights activists, entrepreneurs chasing Big Tech margins, and national-security hawks worried about dependence on US platforms. The useful model is not ideological agreement, but overlapping incentives around interoperability, local control, and reduced platform leverage.

  • Coalitions can be operationally powerful even when their members disagree on almost everything except the immediate target.
  • Tariffs, platform dependence, and anticircumvention rules turn interoperability from a rights issue into an economic and sovereignty issue.
  • Digital public goods become more plausible when activists, investors, and state-security interests temporarily point in the same direction.

item.title: [Reddit Programming] The Consensus Trap
item.description:
This failure-mode note describes how inclusive consultation turns into stalled decisions when no one owns the call. The operational fix is simple and sharp: name a decision owner, time-box input, separate consultation from approval, and accept that not every stakeholder will be fully satisfied.

  • Endless alignment is often unclear decision rights disguised as responsible process.
  • A DRI model works only when the owner can actually decide after consultation.
  • Decision records are useful delivery hygiene, but they do not replace ownership.

item.title: [Reddit Programming] Figma's next-generation data caching platform
item.description:
Figma describes FigCache, a stateless RESP proxy and first-party client layer that turns a fragmented Redis estate into a unified cache data plane. The design is valuable because it moves connection pooling, routing, observability, failover handling, and guardrails below application code while keeping migration reversible.

  • Redis became a critical-path dependency, so Figma inserted a proxy layer to absorb connection volatility and topology changes.
  • The proxy parses structured Redis commands, supports composable routing engines, and can fan out eligible cross-shard read pipelines.
  • The rollout used first-party wrappers, feature flags, incremental domains, production stress tests, and a low-friction endpoint change.

item.title: [Reddit Programming] Should I Run Plain Docker Compose in Production in 2026?
item.description:
The post gives a practical boundary for production Docker Compose: it can work for single-node and customer-managed deployments, but only if operators deliberately close the gaps around orphan containers, disk cleanup, log rotation, health recovery, image pinning, socket exposure, and updates.

  • Compose reconciles a host once; it is not a scheduler or control plane.
  • `--remove-orphans`, image pruning, log caps, pinned images, and socket controls are operational requirements, not polish.
  • The correct comparison is not Compose versus Kubernetes in the abstract, but the host's actual reliability and maintenance surface.

item.title: [Reddit Programming] Formatting an entire 25 million line codebase overnight: the rubyfmt story
item.description:
Stripe's rubyfmt rollout is a large-scale developer-productivity case study: the formatter had to be fast enough for editor use, correct enough for a huge Ruby codebase, and deployable through a coordinated all-repo migration rather than endless piecemeal formatting churn.

  • Formatter adoption at monorepo scale is an organizational migration, not only a parser problem.
  • Correctness validation, blame-ignore support, and rollout coordination matter as much as formatting style.
  • A single consistent tool can remove recurring review noise, but the migration needs explicit ownership and timing.

item.title: [Reddit Programming] AI likes deep modules
item.description:
The essay applies Ousterhout's deep-module idea to AI-assisted development: splitting code is not enough if modules leak Kubernetes objects, complex structs, or other implementation details through shallow wrappers. Deep interfaces absorb complexity so both humans and LLMs have less system state to hold.

  • A module is deep when a small interface hides substantial implementation complexity.
  • Shallow wrappers can increase complexity by moving boilerplate without reducing what callers must know.
  • AI-generated code needs especially clear module boundaries because models tend to reproduce visible surface complexity.

item.title: [Reddit Programming] Architecture by Autocomplete
item.description:
The post argues that AI-generated code often flattens domain concepts into primitive strings, numbers, dates, and maps because that is the statistical average of training data. The review heuristic is concrete: count the primitives in the domain core and replace flattened concepts with domain types and smart constructors.

  • Primitive obsession is a useful diagnostic for AI-assisted code that lacks domain theory.
  • Domain types encode negative space: what values and state transitions should be impossible.
  • Reviewing AI output should start with types because they reveal whether the domain model was actually understood.

item.title: [Reddit Programming] on-call is 90% hunting, 10% fixing
item.description:
The thread captures a common incident-response failure mode: the code fix is short, but the real work is reconstructing context across alerts, traces, deploy history, Slack threads, ownership gaps, and prior postmortems. The durable point is that incident knowledge needs to become searchable operational memory, not evaporate after every page.

  • Modern on-call toil often comes from context reconstruction across tools rather than from the final code change.
  • Deploy archaeology, ownership metadata, and service dependency context need to be available during the incident, not only in the postmortem.
  • Postmortems that no one can retrieve during the next incident are documentation theater.

item.title: [Reddit Programming] Struggling with flaky end-to-end tests due to data dependencies
item.description:
The thread surfaces a common test-architecture trap: teams run long end-to-end flows because production data dependencies are hard to model, then every upstream state inconsistency breaks checkout-level confidence. The better direction is deterministic setup, isolated scenario data, and more behavior checks below the full browser/API journey.

  • A checkout test should not need every previous workflow step to succeed just to reach the state under test.
  • Move most behavior checks to API, integration, contract, and component levels; reserve full E2E for a few critical journeys.
  • Use owned seeded data, scenario IDs, reset helpers, and isolated accounts instead of shared mutable QA fixtures.

item.title: [Reddit Programming] Update on "Co-authored-by: Copilot" in commit messages
item.description:
The VS Code issue is a concrete failure case for AI provenance defaults: an attribution setting was changed toward broader Copilot commit trailers, a bug attributed non-Copilot code even with AI disabled, and the team had to revert the default to off. The lesson is about consent, kill switches, release process, and provenance metadata semantics.

  • AI attribution must be opt-in, explicit, and disabled by global AI-off settings.
  • A feature that writes into commit history needs stronger review, tests, and rollout controls than ordinary UI polish.
  • `Co-authored-by` and `Assisted-by` are not interchangeable; provenance labels should match the actual contribution.

item.title: [Reddit Programming] The peril of laziness lost
item.description:
Bryan Cantrill argues that LLMs lack the programmer virtue of laziness: they do not feel the future cost of extra code, weak abstractions, or accumulated cognitive load. The useful framing is that AI should be used in service of simpler systems, not as a throughput machine for generating more surface area.

  • Good abstraction comes from optimizing future human effort, which current LLMs do not experience.
  • Lines of code are a perverse metric when models can generate volume without owning maintenance cost.
  • AI-assisted engineering needs human constraints, taste, and review discipline to keep systems smaller and clearer.

item.title: [Tech] Trustworthy JavaScript for the Open Web
item.description:
Mozilla outlines WAICT, a browser-side integrity and transparency model for web apps where users must not blindly trust the server to deliver safe JavaScript. The key idea is to bind client code to public manifests and logs so selective malicious code delivery becomes detectable and enforceable.

  • End-to-end encrypted web apps still depend on server-delivered JavaScript unless the browser can verify what code was served.
  • Integrity and transparency logs can make targeted malicious builds observable instead of invisible.
  • The Firefox Nightly prototype is early, but the trust model is important for high-sensitivity browser applications.

item.title: [Ux] Experiment Estimation Study: How Well Can 1,391 Guesses Predict A/B Test Effects?
item.description:
GoodUI compares 1,391 professional guesses against real A/B test outcomes and finds that humans are noisy, mildly optimistic estimators. Crowds reduce random error but do not remove shared positive bias, so estimates are useful for prioritization but cannot replace experiments.

  • The median individual estimate missed the real effect by about 2.7 percentage points.
  • People were directionally right about 64% of the time but tended to overestimate positive impact.
  • Aggregating five to ten estimates reduces noise, but systematic bias remains.

item.title: [Visualization] Estimating how much text on the internet is generated
item.description:
FlowingData points to research estimating that roughly 35% of newly published websites by mid-2025 used AI-generated or AI-assisted text. The result is useful because it complicates the usual story: semantic diversity dropped and sentiment became more positive, but the study did not find statistically significant evidence of lower factual accuracy or stylistic diversity.

  • AI-generated or AI-assisted text appears to have become a large share of newly published websites within three years of ChatGPT's launch.
  • The measured effects include lower semantic diversity and more positive sentiment.
  • The study's findings diverge from the common assumption that generated web text necessarily reduces factual accuracy.
